title: From Data-Driven Models to Physical Insight: Vibrational Entropy Governed by Atomic Volume
authors: Shivam Tripathi, Jatin Kawatra, Varun Malviya, Krishna Mehta
abstract: Vibrational entropy plays a central role in determining phase stability and temperature dependent behavior in materials, yet its calculation from first-principles phonon methods remains computationally demanding. In this work, we combine data-driven modeling with physically motivated analysis to develop an efficient and interpretable framework for predicting vibrational entropy. Using a dataset derived from PhononDB, a feedforward neural network trained on Materials Project and composition based descriptors achieves high predictive accuracy, while SHAP analysis identifies atomic volume as the dominant factor governing vibrational entropy. Guided by this insight, simplified analytical models are constructed, revealing a logarithmic dependence of vibrational entropy on atomic volume consistent with lattice dynamical considerations. A logarithmic linear model is shown to provide an accurate and physically interpretable description across the full range of materials.   To extend the analysis to finite temperatures, a temperature dependent formulation is introduced that incorporates T3 scaling at low temperatures and logarithmic dependence at higher temperatures, consistent with Debye and Einstein type behavior. This unified model captures both structural and thermal contributions to vibrational entropy with good accuracy. Overall, the proposed framework demonstrates that vibrational entropy can be predicted using simple, physically meaningful relationships, offering a computationally efficient alternative to full phonon calculations and enabling entropy informed materials screening.
summary: This paper presents a way to bypass expensive phonon calculations by using machine learning and simple analytical models to predict vibrational entropy. By analyzing a large dataset, the authors found that atomic volume is the most important factor governing this property. They developed a new log-linear model and a temperature-dependent framework that captures both low-temperature Debye-like and high-temperature Einstein-like behavior. This approach enables much faster, large-scale screening of materials for thermodynamic stability.
main_problem: Calculating vibrational entropy using first-principles phonon methods is computationally expensive, creating a bottleneck for high-throughput materials discovery.
main_result: The authors developed a highly accurate, interpretable log-linear model and a temperature-dependent formulation that predicts vibrational entropy using atomic volume as the primary descriptor.
method: The study combines a feedforward neural network trained on Materials Project and PhononDB descriptors with SHAP analysis and least-squares regression to derive simplified analytical models.
model_or_system: The study analyzes a dataset of 9,866 crystalline materials, utilizing a single-hidden-layer neural network and a log-linear model relating vibrational entropy to atomic volume.
important_parameters: Atomic volume, temperature (100-500 K), transition temperature (T* = 200 K), and Debye/Einstein temperature regimes.
main_assumptions: The study operates within the harmonic approximation and assumes atomic volume serves as a sufficient effective descriptor for mass and bonding strength.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a parity plot of predicted vs actual S_vib; Figure 2 ranks feature importance via SHAP; Figure 3 compares linear, logarithmic, and log-linear models; Figure 4 shows parity plots for the temperature-dependent model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of phonon calculations, describes the dataset and neural network architecture, uses SHAP analysis to identify atomic volume as the key driver, presents the derivation of a simplified log-linear model, introduces a temperature-dependent formulation, and validates the models via parity plots and error analysis.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
